one_line_takeaway: CaMeL defeats prompt injection by wrapping an untrusted LLM in a capability-tracking Python interpreter that enforces control- and data-flow security policies, achieving near-zero attack success on AgentDojo (Gemini 2.5 Pro: 300 to 0 attacks) for a modest ~2.8x token cost and ~7-point utility drop (84% to 77%), without retraining the model.
key_finding: CaMeL provides security BY DESIGN with only modest utility cost. In AgentDojo it solves 77% of tasks with provable security versus 84% for an undefended system (abstract headline). On security, with NO extra policies it reduces successful attacks dramatically - e.g. Gemini 2.5 Pro drops from 300 successful attacks (native) to 0 with CaMeL; the residual non-zero ASR comes from two injection tasks (one banking, one travel) that lie explicitly OUTSIDE CaMeL's threat model (the travel one is not even a prompt injection - it is the model print-ing an untrusted review to the user). In the head-to-head baseline comparison on Claude 3.5 Sonnet, CaMeL had 0 successful attacks while the next-best defense (tool filter) had 8; GPT-4o-mini with the native instruction-hierarchy API suffered 276 attacks but 0 under CaMeL. Overhead is 2.82x input and 2.73x output tokens for the median task under attack. A cheaper Q-LLM barely hurts utility (Claude 3.5 Haiku as Q-LLM with Claude 4 Sonnet P-LLM: ~1% utility drop for ~12% median cost reduction).
core_contribution: The paper introduces CaMeL (CApabilities for MachinE Learning), a system-level defense that neutralizes prompt injection in LLM agents WITHOUT modifying or retraining the underlying model. CaMeL is presented as the first concrete instantiation of Willison's Dual-LLM pattern: a Privileged LLM (P-LLM) sees only the trusted user query and emits pseudo-Python code expressing the control flow, while a Quarantined LLM (Q-LLM) parses untrusted tool outputs into structured data but has no tool access. The paper's own novel contributions beyond the cited Dual-LLM idea are: (1) a custom restricted-Python interpreter (built on Python's ast library) that executes the P-LLM's code, maintains a per-value data-flow/dependency graph, and enforces security policies before each tool call; (2) a capability system attaching metadata (provenance sources + allowed readers) to every value so that even correctly-planned actions cannot exfiltrate data over unauthorized flows; and (3) security policies expressed as arbitrary Python functions returning Allowed/Denied. The paper explicitly distinguishes control-flow hijacking (which plain Dual-LLM already blocks) from data-flow manipulation (changing tool ARGUMENTS, analogous to SQL parameter injection), which CaMeL additionally stops via capabilities.
threat_model: Primary threat: prompt injection from untrusted data sources in agentic tool-calling systems. An adversary A plants specially-crafted data (an email, document, spreadsheet, web page, review) that the victim's agent later retrieves; the injected text tries to divert the data flow (e.g., changing a 'send email' recipient to attacker@gmail.com and the attached file to confidential.txt) or otherwise cause an unsafe action. Formalized as the PI-SEC security game: A supplies an adversarial memory state mem*, the Agent produces a Trace of (tool, args, mem) tuples, and A wins if any action falls outside the prompt's allowed-action set Omega_prompt. ASSUMPTIONS: the user prompt is trusted (user is not pasting untrusted prompts), and any agent memory is uncompromised. Explicit NON-GOALS: attacks that affect neither control nor data flow (e.g., text-to-text attacks that mis-summarize an email, or prompt-injection-induced phishing such as 'click this link'); CaMeL also does not aim for fully autonomous, human-free operation. Secondary threat models CaMeL can help with (Section 8): rogue/compromised insider users exfiltrating data, and maliciously installed 'spy' tools whose documentation prompt-injects the agent.
limitations: The authors are candid about several limits. (1) Side channels (Section 7): even with CaMeL, an adversary can leak private data via indirect dependencies (loop-count image fetches), via exception-triggering that reveals one bit (conditional NotEnoughInformationError / IndexError), and via timing; STRICT mode mitigates the first two by propagating dependencies, and the time module is simply absent, but the authors do not claim to have closed all side channels. (2) The 'Data requires action' failure is inherent to the Dual-LLM pattern - the P-LLM cannot plan over data it is not allowed to read (e.g. 'do the actions specified in this email'), and no evaluated model solved such tasks. (3) The Q-LLM cannot tell the P-LLM WHAT information it is missing (that channel would itself be an injection vector), causing 'not enough context' failures. (4) When data legitimately becomes control flow (Section 6.4, 'monitor emails and do what they say'), isolation alone fails and one must rely on policies; adversaries can also split a malicious program into benign-looking sub-instructions. (5) Utility can degrade notably on poorly-documented APIs (Travel suite). (6) No atomicity/rollback for tool calls with side effects when later code errors. (7) Security policies are currently authored by the CaMeL engine operator, not yet multi-party. Residual ASR is non-zero but attributed to explicit non-goals.

datasets_used: AgentDojo agentic security benchmark (Debenedetti et al. 2024b), suites: Workspace, Banking, Travel, Slack
949 total prompt-injection attacks across AgentDojo tasks
quantitative_results: 77% of tasks solved with provable security under CaMeL vs 84% with an undefended system (AgentDojo, abstract)
Gemini 2.5 Pro successful attacks: 300 (native tool-calling) -> 0 (with CaMeL)
Baseline security on Claude 3.5 Sonnet: CaMeL 0 successful attacks vs tool filter 8 (next best), out of 949 attacks
GPT-4o-mini: 276 successful attacks with native instruction-hierarchy API -> 0 with CaMeL
Token overhead: 2.82x input tokens and 2.73x output tokens for the median task under attack (Claude 3.5 Sonnet)
Cheaper Q-LLM (Claude 3.5 Haiku Q-LLM + Claude 4 Sonnet P-LLM): ~1% utility drop for ~12% median-task cost reduction
Travel-suite utility for the Claude Sonnet family with unchanged CaMeL: 25% (3.5) -> 55% (3.7) -> 75% (4)
Spotlighting baseline overhead: 1.06x input / 0.98x output tokens (lowest among compared defenses)

Claude failure breakdown (Table 1): 62 correct total; 13 underdocumented-API failures (all in Travel); 5 not-enough-context-for-Q-LLM; 5 data-requires-action; 3 wrong-assumptions; 3 Q-LLM-overdoes-it; 3 AgentDojo bugs; 1 ambiguous; 2 query-misunderstanding (caption-confirmed counts: query-misunderstanding=2, wrong-assumptions=3, not-enough-context=5)
baselines_compared: Instruction hierarchy via GPT-4o-mini (Wallace et al. 2024 / OpenAI 2024)
